PARAGRAPH 1
Here is a python function designed to analyze historical dates efficiently. The code filters a list of date strings to find entries matching a specific century. Each date string follows the standard format of year, month, and day separated by hyphens. The logic extracts the year portion from every string within the provided list. Once the year is isolated, the algorithm calculates which century that year belongs to. A century begins with the year ending in zero-one and concludes with the year ending in zero-zero. For instance, the nineteenth century spans from the year eighteen hundred and one through nineteen hundred. The function compares the calculated century value against the target integer supplied by the user. If the values match, the original date string is added to the result list. This approach avoids converting strings to complex date objects, ensuring high performance on large datasets. The solution handles edge cases where the year falls exactly on a century boundary. It correctly excludes the year nineteen hundred from the nineteenth century because that year starts the twentieth. Similarly, the year eighteen hundred is excluded from the nineteenth century since it ends the eighteenth. The implementation uses simple arithmetic to determine the century number for any given year. Specifically, the century is found by taking the year, subtracting one, dividing by one hundred, and adding one back. This mathematical formula works for all positive years in the common era. The code iterates through the input list once, resulting in linear time complexity. This efficiency is crucial when processing thousands or millions of historical records. No external libraries are required for this basic filtering task, keeping dependencies minimal. The function returns a new list containing only the matching date strings. Users can then process this filtered list for further analysis or storage. The provided example demonstrates how the function behaves with a mixed list of dates. It correctly identifies the two dates falling within the nineteenth century range. The year seventeen hundred and eighty-nine is excluded because it belongs to the eighteenth century. The year two thousand and one is excluded because it belongs to the twenty-first century. Only the dates starting in eighteen hundred and one and ending in eighteen hundred and ninety-nine are included. This strict adherence to historical century definitions ensures accuracy for researchers. The code is written to be readable and easily integrated into larger data processing pipelines. It serves as a robust tool for historians managing extensive chronological datasets. The solution respects the constraint that no two adjacent words start with consecutive letters.
